coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as promising nanomaterials for bioimaging-guided photodynamic therapy (PDT). However, designing red-emissive CDs (RCDs) with tunable type I and type II reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation to simultaneously meet PDT applications in aerobic and hypoxic scenarios still remain major challenges. Herein, three types of RCDs with maximum emission at approximately 680 nm are successfully prepared. It is noteworthy that they exhibit the adjustable ROS production with equal superoxide anion (via type I PDT) and incremental singlet oxygen (via type II PDT). Detailed structural and optical characterizations along with theoretical calculation reveal that the unique type I/II ROS formation mainly depends on the core sizes and surface states of RCDs, which determine their identical redox potentials and tapering energy gaps between singlet- and triplet states, respectively. Additionally, due to the inherent mitochondria targeting capability, RCDs enable themselves to induce cell programmed death via activating mitochondrion-mediated apoptotic pathways. This work exploits the unprecedented RCDs with tunable type I and type II ROS generation that could ensure highly efficient tumor eradication both in vitro and in vivo, even under the harsh tumor microenvironment, providing a new prospect for CDs as nanophotosensitizers to conquer the limitations of single type PDT.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of RCDs structures and photophysical properties. TEM image, HRTEM image, and size distribution of the prepared h-RCDs (a), e-RCDs (b), and p-RCDs (c). (d) FTIR spectra of the RCDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (e), O1s (f), and N1s (g) of the RCDs. (h) Absorbance spectra of the RCDs. (i) Normalized emission spectra (λex = 635 nm) of the RCDs.

Verification of ROS generation from RCDs in solution. (a) Degradation curves comparison of DPBF absorption at 416 nm in the presence of RCDs. (b) Relative intensity comparison of DHE fluorescence at 605 nm in the presence of RCDs. (c) Degradation curves comparison of ABDA absorption at 380 nm in the presence of RCDs. Degradation curves of ABDA absorption in the presence of h-RCDs (d), e-RCDs (e), and p-RCDs (f). (g) ESR spectra of the RCDs with DMPO as O2 −• trapper. (h) ESR spectra of the RCDs with TEMP as 1O2 trapper. (i) Schematic diagram of type I and II ROSs produced by the three RCDs.

Mechanism analysis of type I/II ROSs generated by RCDs. Fluorescence spectrum and 40 ms delayed phosphorescence spectra of (a) h-RCDs, (b) e-RCDs, and (c) p-RCDs at 77 K. The fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of the RCDs measured at 77 K respectively correspond to the S1 and T1 levels. (d) Energy levels and ΔE S1-T1 values of the RCDs. (e) Proposed strategy for efficient 1O2 generation by reducing the singlet−triplet energy gap. (f) UPS spectra of RCDs. (g) Band structures of RCDs. (h) Quasi-RCDs structures for evaluating ΔE S1-T1 trends by TD-DFT.

ROS generation and PDT of RCDs in vitro. (a) CSLM images of RCDs-treated 4T1 cells co-stained with DCFH-DA, DHE, and SOSG as the ROS, O2 −•, and 1O2 indicators, respectively. Scale bars: 10 μm. (green channel: 500–530 nm, excited at 488 nm; red channel: 568–643 nm; excited at 543 nm). (b) CSLM images of living and dead 4T1 cells after PDT with RCDs. Scale bars: 20 μm. (green channel: 500–530 nm, excited at 488 nm; red channel: 568–643 nm; excited at 543 nm). (c) Relative viabilities of RCDs incubated 4T1 cells with or without 635 nm laser irradiation. (d) Flow cytometry analysis of RCDs-treated 4T1 cells with 635 nm laser irradiation. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Mechanism of mitochondrial dysfunction induced tumor cell apoptosis. (a) CLSM images of p-RCDs incubated 4T1 cells co-stained with Lyso-Tracker green. Scale bars: 10 μm. (green channel: 500–533 nm, excited at 488 nm; red channel: 650–710 nm; excited at 637 nm). (b) CLSM images of p-RCDs incubated 4T1 cells co-stained with Mito-Tracker green. Scale bars: 10 μm. (green channel: 500–533 nm, excited at 488 nm; red channel: 650–710; excited at 637 nm). (c) CLSM imaging of JC-1 stained 4T1 cells after PDT with RCDs. Scale bars: 10 μm. (green channel: 500–533 nm, excited at 488 nm; red channel: 568–643 nm; excited at 488 nm). (d) CLSM imaging of the cytochrome c released from the mitochondria of 4T1 cells after PDT with RCDs. Scale bars: 5 μm. (green channel: 500–533 nm, excited at 488 nm; red channel: 568–643 nm; excited at 543 nm). (e) Western blot analysis of the related protein expression of the mitochondrion-mediated apoptosis pathway in 4T1 cells. (f) Quantitative analysis of the related protein expression of the mitochondrion-mediated apoptosis pathway in 4T1 cells. (g) Schematic diagram of the mitochondrion-mediated cell apoptosis induced by PDT with RCDs. P values were calculated by one-way ANOVA (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, and ***P < 0.001). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Fluorescence imaging and PDT of RCDs in vivo. (a) Fluorescence imaging after intratumoral injection of p-RCDs. (b) Photographs of tumor bearing mice on the 15th day after different treatments with laser irradiation. (c) Average tumor weights in different treatment groups. (d) Relative tumor volume curves of 4T1 tumor-bearing mice after different treatments within 15 days. (e) H&E stained images and TUNEL (green) immunofluorescence of tumor tissues. Scale bar: 200 μm. (f) Time-dependent body-weight curves of 4T1 tumor-bearing mice after different treatments within 15 days. P values were calculated by one-way ANOVA (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, and ***P < 0.001). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Illustration of RCDs as nanoPSs with tunable ROS generation. (a) Preparation process of the type I/II RCDs nanoPSs with equal O2 −• and incremental 1O2 production. (b) Mechanism of type I and II ROSs produced by the prepared RCDs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as promising nanomaterials for bioimaging-guided photodynamic therapy (PDT). However, designing red-emissive CDs (RCDs) with tunable type I and type II reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation to simultaneously meet PDT applications in aerobic and hypoxic scenarios still remain major challenges. Herein, three types of RCDs with maximum emission at approximately 680 nm are successfully prepared. It is noteworthy that they exhibit the adjustable ROS production with equal superoxide anion (via type I PDT) and incremental singlet oxygen (via type II PDT). Detailed structural and optical characterizations along with theoretical calculation reveal that the unique type I/II ROS formation mainly depends on the core sizes and surface states of RCDs, which determine their identical redox potentials and tapering energy gaps between singlet- and triplet states, respectively. Additionally, due to the inherent mitochondria targeting capability, RCDs enable themselves to induce cell programmed death via activating mitochondrion-mediated apoptotic pathways. This work exploits the unprecedented RCDs with tunable type I and type II ROS generation that could ensure highly efficient tumor eradication both in vitro and in vivo, even under the harsh tumor microenvironment, providing a new prospect for CDs as nanophotosensitizers to conquer the limitations of single type PDT.

Red emissive carbon dots with tunable reactive oxygen species generation are prepared to achieve highly efficient fluorescence imaging-guided type I/II photodynamic therapy via activating mitochondrion-mediated apoptotic pathways of cancer cells, providing a new prospect for carbon dots as nanophotosensitizers to conquer the limitations of single type PDT.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a clinically approved cancer treatment with spatiotemporal selectivity, accurate controllability, and minimal invasiveness, photodynamic therapy (PDT) refers to using photosensitizers (PSs) activated by appropriate light to generate toxic reactive oxygen species (ROS) to oxidatively damage cancer cells [1,2]. The majority of currently reported PSs are based on the O2 dependent type II path through the generation of 1O2 with the energy transfer from the excited triplet state (T1) of PSs to O2 [3,4]. However, the hypoxia (pO2 < 5 mmHg) exhibited in larger tumors drastically weakens the anticancer outcome [5,6]. Compared with type II PDT, excited type I PSs transfer electrons to surrounding substrates and O2 to yield O2 −•, H2O2, and •OH, showing a low O2 dependent performance [7,8]. Nonetheless, the weak oxidative cytotoxicity of O2 −• and H2O2 and harsh generation conditions of •OH (e.g. UV light and low pH) are often criticized [9,10]. To sum up, type II ROS is mainly suitable for use when oxygen is sufficient, while type I is more proper for hypoxic environment. To meet the demand of PDT usage in a wide clinical scenarios, PSs with both type I and type II ROSs generation is highly desired to be developed [11–13].

Carbon dots (CDs) with superior optical properties and remarkable photoactive therapeutic performance have inevitably attracted widespread attention in anticancer, particularly for bioimaging-guided PDT [14–18]. Currently, the major developments in CDs for anticancer PDT are: 1) type II CDs with high 1O2 generation [19], 2) magnetofluorescent CDs as oxygenerator to regulate tumor hypoxia for enhanced type II PDT [20,21], 3) CDs as PS carriers for synergetic PDT [22,23], 4) highly crystalline Zn-CDs with efficient O2 −• production for type I PDT [24], etc. Although making considerable progress in PDT, studies on the preparation of CDs to achieve the tunable ROS generation for type I and II PDT have not been reported so far. Given the relatively low absorption/scattering of skin, tissue, blood, and water in the transparency window (600–950 nm) [7,25–27], it is urgently needed to select suitable precursors and synthetic conditions for realizing red-emissive CDs (RCDs) with both type I and II ROSs generation and understanding the ROS capabilitis of CDs related to their structures to achieve on-demand regulation of type I and II PDT.

Mitochondria as a central hub of catabolic and anabolic metabolisms, such as adenosine triphosphate (ATP) biogenesis, amino acid biosynthesis, and fatty acid oxidation, plays a pivotal role in cell death through various programmed and non-programmed pathways, and has been regarded as a popular target in organelle-targeted therapy, especially for PDT. The mitochondrial dysfunction induced by ROS can interrupt energy supply and subsequently activate mitochondrion-mediated apoptotic pathways [28]. Moreover, mitochondria were shown to have higher oxygen content, especially under hypoxic environment, which is favorable for PDT [29]. Evidently, exploiting novel type I/II CDs nanoPSs that are capable of mitochondrion targeting would be of great importance for obtaining superior PDT effect.

Hypericum perforatum, as medicinal natural agents, has been proved to be rich in PS hypericin and considered to a robust diagnostic and therapeutic tool for tumor treatment [30]. In this paper, three types of hypericum perforatum extract-derived RCDs with tunable ROS generation were successfully prepared, as shown in Scheme 1 a. The synthesized h-RCDs can simultaneously produce detectable 1O2 (0.02) and a certain amount of O2 −•. By changing reactive solvents, the 1O2 quantum yields of the RCDs could be regulated to 0.18 (e-RCDs) and 0.42 (p-RCDs), whereas their O2 −• production remained equal. The photosensitive regulation mechanism of these type I/II RCDs was preliminary illustrated that their identical redox potentials resulted in equal O2 −• production, and the tapering energy gap between the singlet state (S1) and T1 was responsible for their sequentially enhanced 1O2 generation (Scheme 1b). Moreover, due to the inherent mitochondria targeting ability of RCDs, an in-depth investigation of RCDs in PDT was also carried out. In vitro and in vivo experiments indicated that the well-designed RCDs could be used as novel nanoPSs for deep red fluorescence imaging-guided type I/II PDT and achieve a highly efficient anticancer outcome even under the hypoxic conditions. To the best of our knowledge, this study was the first to synthesize type I/II RCD nanoPSs with tunable ROS generation, investigate the regulation mechanism related to their fundamental strutures, and explore the tumor cell death pathway in detail.

Hypericum perforatum extract was obtained from Xi'an Ruidi-Bio Technology co., Ltd. Polyethylenimine (PEI) was obtained from Aladdin Company (Shanghai, China). 3-(4-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-diphenylte-trazolium bromide (MTT) assay kits, Annexin V-FITC/PI Apoptosis Detection Kit, Dihydroethidium (DHE), Calcein-AM/PI kits, Singlet oxygen sensor green (SOSG), and 5,5′,6,6′-tetrachloro-1,1′,3,3′-tetraethylbenzimidazolylcarbocyanine iodide (JC-1) assay kit were obtained from Beyotime Biotechnology Company (Beijing, China). 9,10-Anthracenediyl-bis(methylene) Dimalonic Acid (ABDA) and 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) were obtained from Merck (Germany). The hypoxic experiments in vitro was carried out by using microaerophilic AnaeroPack produced by Mtsubishi Gas Chemical Company, Inc. Oxygen indicator was used to detect the oxygen levels in the chamber (O2% ≤ 1%). 3′-p-(hydroxyphenyl) fluorescein (HPF) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Mito-Tracker™ Green FM and Lyso-Tracker™ Green DND-26 were obtained from Thermo Fisher Scientific (USA). The antibodies including anti-Cleaved-caspase 3 Ab (Asp 175), anti-GAPDH (D16H11), anti-Cleaved-caspase 9 Ab (Asp 315), and anti PARP Ab were obtained from Cell Signaling Technology (USA). All commercially available chemicals and materials were used without further purification unless otherwise stated.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were performed on HT7700 and a JEM-2100F field-emission electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were recorded on an Excalibur 3100 spectrophotometer (Varian, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses and Ultra-violet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) were obtained on an ESCALAB 250Xi analysis system (Thermo Instruments Inc., U.S.). UV–vis absorption spectra were acquired on a U-3900 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra were obtained from F-4600 spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured by using an X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany) with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.5178 Å).

In brief, 40 mg of Hypericum perforatum extract were dissolved in 40 mL of deionized (DI) water and then sealed in a 50 mL of Teflon autoclave heating for 6 h at 160 °C. After cooling down to room temperature, the obtained h-RCDs were filtered through a Millipore 0.22 μm filter paper and then dialyzed (molecular weight cut-off: 3500 Da) with agitation for three days. Subsequently, the h-RCDs were dried using freezer dryer and resuspended in water for follow-on operational test and evaluation. Differently, the solvent for the preparation of e-RCDs was a mixed solvent of 30 mL of DI water and 10 mL of ethyl alcohol, while that of p-RCDs was a mixed solvent of 30 mL of DI water and 10 mL of ethyl alcohol containing 0.4 mg of PEI.

DPBF was used to measure the ROS production of RCDs. The absorption of the RCDs in DMF at 635 nm was adjusted to about 0.15 OD. 60 μL of DPBF solution (DMF, 1 mg/mL) was added to the above 2 mL of RCDs solution, and then irradiated by a 635 nm laser with 50 mW cm−2. The data were collected every 10 s until 60 s. Afterwards, the absorption intensity at 416 nm was collated and plotted against irradiation time. The decrease of DPBF absorption along time is proportional to its reaction rate with ROS production.

DHE was used to detect the capacity of O2 •- generation of RCDs. With the O2 •- production, it can oxidize DHE to form ethidium, which further reacts with DNA and emits fluorescence at ∼605 nm. The absorption of the RCDs in DMF at 635 nm was adjusted to about 0.15 OD. Then, 10 μL of DHE solution (DMF, 10 mM) and 10 μL of ctDNA solution (PBS, 10 mg/mL) were added into the RCDs solution. The mixture was irradiated with 635 nm light at 50 mW cm−2. The fluorescence change was collected every minute until 5 min by the fluorescence spectrometer.

ABDA was used to detect the 1O2 production of RCDs in solution. With the 1O2 production, it would oxidize the ABDA, leading to the gradual reduction of its absorption at 378 nm. The absorption of the RCDs in DI water at 635 nm was adjusted to about 0.15 OD. Then, 60 μL of ABDA solution (DMSO, 1 mg/mL) was added into the above 2 mL of RCDs solution. The mixture was irradiated with 635 nm light at 50 mW cm−2. The decreasing absorbance of ABDA was recorded every 30 s until 5 min by the UV–Vis absorption spectrophotometer. The change of ABDA absorption at about 380 nm was collated and plotted against irradiation time. The 1O2 quantum yield of RCDs was calculated according to the following equation: Φx = Φstd × (Sx/S std). The x and std refer to the unknown sample and standard sample, respectively. S refers to the slope of the absorbance degradation of ABDA. Methylene (MB) was selected to a standard PS to calculate the 1O2 quantum yield. ΦMB is given as 0.52.

Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy was applied to monitor the 1O2 generation using 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) as spin-trap agent. After capturing 1O2 by TEMP, the stable nitroxide radical TEMPO can be formed. The absorption of the RCDs in DMF at 635 nm was adjusted to about 0.15 OD. Then, 50 μL of TEMP (4 mM) was added to RCDs solution. The mixture was irradiated by the 635 nm laser with 50 mW cm−2 for 10 min. ESR spectra of spin were monitored in a range of 3460–3560 G. Background interference was collected using the sample before irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of O2 •- production by ESR
ce:section.ce:para: ESR spectroscopy was applied to monitor the O2 −• generation using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) as spin-trap agent. With the O2 −• production, it would react with DMPO to generate DMPO-OOH. The absorption of the RCDs in water at 635 nm was adjusted to about 0.15 OD. Then, 10 μL of DMPO (0.2 M) was added to RCDs solution. The mixture was irradiated by the 635 nm laser with 50 mW cm−2 for 10 min. ESR spectra of spin were monitored in a range of 3460–3560 G. Background interference was collected using the sample before irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ROS generation in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: 4T1 cells were seeded in 35 mm confocal dishes and incubated for 12 h. After washing with PBS, the cells were incubated with RPMI 1640 medium containing 200 μg mL−1 of RCDs at 37 °C, 5% CO2 for 12 h. Then, 1 mL of the fresh medium was replaced containing 2 μM DCFH-DA. With further incubation for 0.5 h, the cells were subjected to be irradiated by 635 nm laser (50 mW cm−2) for 5 min. The bright green fluorescence was captured by a Nikon C1si laser scanning confocal microscopy (λex = 488 nm) with a 60 × objective lens. In addition, the dark toxicity of RCDs was also carried out by the above procedure without laser irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of O2 •- generation in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: 4T1 cells were seeded in 35 mm confocal dishes and incubated for 12 h under normoxia or hypoxia. After washing with PBS, the cells were incubated with RPMI 1640 medium containing 200 μg mL−1 of RCDs at 37 °C, 5% CO2 for 12 h. Then, the fresh medium was added containing 50 μM DHE. With further incubation for 0.5 h, the cells were subjected to be irradiated for 635 nm (50 mW cm−2) for 5 min. The bright red fluorescence was captured by a Nikon C1si laser scanning confocal microscopy (λex = 488 nm) with a 60 × objective lens. In addition, the dark toxicity of RCDs was also carried out by the above procedure without laser irradiation.

4T1 cells were seeded in 35 mm confocal dishes and incubated for 12 h under normoxia or hypoxia. After washing with PBS, the cells were incubated with RPMI 1640 medium containing 200 μg mL−1 of RCDs at 37 °C, 5% CO2 for 12 h. Then, the cells were fixed with cold methanol for 2 min and further incubated with SOSG (10 μM) for 20 min. With incubation for 0.5 h, the cells were subjected to be irradiated by 635 nm (50 mW cm−2) for 5 min. The bright green fluorescence was captured by a Nikon C1si laser scanning confocal microscopy (λex = 488 nm) with a 60 × objective lens. In addition, the dark toxicity of RCDs was also carried out by the above procedure without laser irradiation.

4T1 cells were seeded in 35 mm confocal dishes and incubated for 12 h under normoxia or hypoxia. After washing with PBS, the cells were incubated with RPMI 1640 medium containing 200 μg mL−1 of RCDs at 37 °C, 5% CO2 for 12 h. Then, the fresh medium was added containing 10 μM HPF. With incubation for 0.5 h, the cells were subjected to be irradiated by 635 nm (50 mW cm−2) for 5 min. The bright green fluorescence was captured by a Nikon C1si laser scanning confocal microscopy (λex = 488 nm) with a 60 × objective lens. In addition, the dark toxicity of RCDs was also carried out by the above procedure without laser irradiation.

4T1 cells were seeded in 35 mm confocal dishes and incubated for 12 h. After washing with PBS, the cells were incubated with RPMI 1640 medium containing 200 μg mL−1 of RCDs at 37 °C, 5% CO2 for 12 h, followed by exposure to 635 nm (100 mW cm−2) for 10 min. After incubation at 37 °C for 12 h, the fresh medium was added containing 5 μM calcein AM and 5 μM propidium iodide (PI) with incubation for 0.5 h. Then the bright green/red fluorescence was captured by a Nikon C1si laser scanning confocal microscopy (λex = 488/543 nm) with a 10 × objective lens. In addition, the dark toxicity of RCDs was also carried out by the above procedure without laser irradiation.

4T1 cells were seeded in a 96-well plate at a density of 5 × 104 per well and cultured in 5% CO2 at 37 °C for 24 h under normoxia or hypoxia. Then, the 4T1 cells were incubated with RCDs at different concentrations (0, 50, 100, 150, and 200 μg mL−1) for 12 h, followed by exposure to 635 nm (100 mW cm−2) for 10 min. After incubation at 37 °C for 12 h, the standard MTT assay was carried out to calculate the relative cell viability (%) by comparing to the control untreated cells. Values represent the mean of six independent experiments. In addition, the dark toxicity of RCDs was also carried out by the above procedure without laser irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Apoptosis detected by flow cytometry and CLSM
ce:section.ce:para: For flow cytometry analysis, 4T1 cells were seeded on 6 well plates and incubated for 12 h. After washing with PBS, the cells were incubated with RPMI 1640 medium containing 200 μg mL−1 of RCDs at 37 °C, 5% CO2 for 12 h. Then, the cells were subjected to be irradiated by 635 nm (100 mW cm−2) for 10 min. With further incubation 6 h, the cells were collected and treated with AnnexinV-FITC/PI cell apoptosis detection kit. For confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) analysis, the treatment and staining were same as the flow cytometry. Then, the bright green/red fluorescence in 4T1 cells was captured by a Nikon C1si laser scanning confocal microscopy.

4T1 cells were seeded in 35 mm confocal dishes and incubated for 12 h. After washing with PBS, the cells were incubated with RPMI 1640 medium containing 50 μg mL−1 of RCDs at 37 °C, 5% CO2 for 12 h. Then, the cells were imaged by a Nikon C1si laser scanning confocal microscopy (λex = 637 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of cell uptake and targeting
ce:section.ce:para: 4T1 cells were seeded in 35 mm confocal dishes and incubated for 12 h. After washing with PBS, the cells were incubated with RPMI 1640 medium containing 50 μg mL−1 of RCDs. At 3 or 8 h, the cells were taken from the incubator and washed twice with PBS, and the mitochondrion (Mito-Tracker™ Green FM) and lysosome (Lyso-Tracker™ Green DND-26) fluorescence probe were added into the confocal dishes with 100 nM and incubated for 0.5 h, respectively. Then, the cells were imaged by a Nikon C1si laser scanning confocal microscopy. The Pearson's correlation coefficients were obtained from the software of Nikon A1R confocal microscope.

4T1 cells were seeded on 6 well plate and incubated for 12 h. After washing with PBS, the cells were incubated with RPMI 1640 medium containing 200 μg mL−1 of RCDs at 37 °C, 5% CO2 for 12 h. After irradiation with 635 nm (100 mW cm−2) for 10 min, the cells were treated with JC-1 dye (5 μg mL−1) for 30 min away from light. After washing with PBS, the bright green/red fluorescence was captured by a Nikon C1si laser scanning confocal microscopy (λex = 488 nm, for the green channel; λex = 543 nm, for the red channel) with a 60 × objective lens.

4T1 cells were seeded on 6 well plate and incubated for 12 h. After washing with PBS, the cells were incubated with RPMI 1640 medium containing 200 μg mL−1 of RCDs at 37 °C, 5% CO2 for 12 h. Then, the cells were subjected to be irradiated by 635 nm (100 mW cm−2) for 10 min. After further incubation overnight, the cells were collected and lysed with loading buffer, and heated for 5 min at 99 °C. The lysis was centrifuged for 10 min with 12 000 rpm. The supernatant was collected and loaded in 12% SDS-PAGE gel, and transferred to PVDF membranes with 200 mA for 40 min. Subsequently, the membrane was washed with TBST for three times and blocked with bovine serum albumin or skim milk powder for 2 h at room temperature. With soaking in the blocking solution (1:500 dilution) containing primary antibodies at 4 °C overnight, the membrane was washed three times by TBST for 5 min each time. After further treatment with secondary antibody (1:3000 dilution) at room temperature for 1 h, the membrane was similarly washed three times by TBST for 5 min each time. The expression of proteins was observed by chemiluminescence HRP substrate.

All animal experiments were reviewed and approved by Animal Ethics Committee of Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry (approval ID: LHDW-210114). Female BALB/c nude mice, 6–8 weeks old, were purchased from Beijing Weitong Lihua Laboratory Animal Technology Co., LTD. All animal experiments were conducted at Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. For in vivo imaging, mice were injected intratumorally 50 μL RCDs (2 mg/mL). The excitation wavelength at 620 nm, and the collected fluorescence channels were at 710 nm. In vivo imaging system were measured by IVIS Lumina III.

To assess and compare the efficacy of RCDs in vivo upon photoactivation, BALB/c nude mice bearing 4T1 tumor cells was established by inoculating subcutaneously on the right flanks of mice. After 7 days, the tumor volume around 80 mm3 of mice were randomly divided into 4 groups: (I) PBS control, (II) h-RCDs, (III) e-RCDs, and (IV) p-RCDs. Mice were injected intratumorally with equivalent dose 50 μL RCDs (2 mg/mL) of RCDs. After 8 h, mice were anaesthetized and irradiated with 635 nm laser (100 mW cm−2, 10 min). The tumor volume (width2 × length)/2 of mice were monitored every two days. The mice were sacrificed on 15th day and the tumor were removed, weighed, and photographed.

The female BALB/c nude mice treated with saline and the RCDs (50 μL, 2 mg/mL) were used to evaluate the biocompatible analysis. After 15 d, the major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney) were excised from the mice for hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining. Meanwhile, the blood samples were collected for blood routine and biochemical analysis.

The related data were representative results from at least three independent experiments and the statistical analyses were carried out by GraphPad Prism Software. Comparison between two groups was performed by Student's t-test. Comparison among multiple groups was performed by one-way ANOVA, and further comparison was performed by LSD-t test. *P < 0.05 indicated that the difference was statistically significant.

Previous reports have shown that changing the solvent during synthesis process is a powerful method to affect the graphitization of core and surface groups of CDs to endow them with different photochemical and photophysical properties, such as fluorescence quantum yield, excitation and emission ranges, etc [31]. In this work, the h-RCDs was synthesized by the hydrothermal method, while e-RCDs and p-RCDs were prepared by changing reactive solvents to 75% (v/v) ethanol + 25% (v/v) water and 75% (v/v) ethanol + 25% (v/v) water + 0.001 (v/v) polyethylenimine (PEI), respectively. Subsequently, we characterized the structure of the prepared three RCDs. The TEM images (Fig. 1 a–c) showed that the three RCDs are spherical and evenly dispersed. The average particle sizes of h-RCDs, e-RCDs, and p-RCDs were measured to be approximately 4.0, 3.9, and 4.0 nm, respectively. Obvious lattice structures could be observed in the HRTEM images of the three RCDs, indicating the high crystallinity. The lattice spacing of 0.21 nm is consistent with the (100) lattice spacing of graphene [32,33]. The XRD patterns of the three RCDs (Fig. S1) have a wide diffraction peak at about 20°, which is assigned to the (002) plane of nanographite [34].

The surface compositions of RCDs were investigated by FTIR spectroscopy. As presented in Fig. 1d, all RCDs have wide peaks at 3470 cm−1, which corresponds to the O–H group on the surface, ensuring the good water dispersity of RCDs. In addition, the same observed peaks at 1020 and 1650 cm−1 can be assigned to C–O and CC stretching [35]. Two stretched bands of the CO group were found: the band at 1730 cm−1 represents the carboxylic acid involved in a H-bond, and the band at 1780 cm−1 is the free form [36]. Distinctively, the CO peak at 1780 cm−1 in e-RCDs could be clearly observed. Moreover, the vibrational modes of CN and C–N = at 1590 and 1350 cm−1 in p-RCDs could be detected. XPS was further performed to evaluate the RCD surfaces. As shown in Fig. S2, three classical peaks centered at 286.3 (C1s), 401.2 (N1s), and 532.7 (O1s) could be observed in the survey spectra of the three RCDs, indicating that the RCDs contained C, N, and O elements. Table S1 showed that the relative contents of O in e-RCDs and p-RCDs were higher than that in h-RCDs, whereas the relative content of N in p-RCDs was slightly higher than those in h-RCDs and e-RCDs. The high-resolution C1s spectra of RCDs were deconvoluted into four peaks, corresponding to sp2 carbons (CC, 284.5 eV), sp3 carbons (C–O/C–N, 286.1 eV), carbonyl carbons (CO/CN, 287.8 eV), and carboxyl carbons (COOH, 289.0 eV) (Fig. 1e) [37,38]. The O1s spectra of RCDs displayed two peaks at 531.6 and 533.0 eV, which corresponded to CO and C–O (Fig. 1f) [33]. The N1s spectra of RCDs were deconvoluted into three peaks at 399.6 eV (pyridinic N), 400.2 eV (pyrrolic N), and 402.1 eV (graphitic N) (Fig. 1g) [39]. Table S2 exhibited the contents of sp2 C in h-RCDs (37.6%), e-RCDs (38.3%), and p-RCDs (39.8%), which were consistent with the particle sizes of RCDs. The result indicated that they had the same conjugated π-domains of carbon cores. However, the high-resolution C1s and O1s XPS spectra suggested that the CO structural motif in the e-RCDs and p-RCDs were apparently higher than that in h-RCDs (Table S3). Upon PEI addition, p-RCDs contain a higher amount of pyridinic N (Table S4), which was consistent with the FTIR results.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of all the RCDs showed the typical π−π* transition of CDs in the region of 200–250 nm [40], as shown in Fig. 1h. The absorbance peak around 500 nm could be assigned to the n–π* electronic transition of CO and CN groups in RCDs (Fig. S3) [41], corresponding to the XPS and FITR analysis. In addition, the three RCDs also exhibited effective absorbance in the range of 600–650 nm. The fluorescence spectra of RCDs indicated that they had the deep-red emission with maximum peak at approximately 680 nm (Fig. 1i).

DPBF was applied as the 1O2 and O2 −•chemical trapping agent and its absorbance changes at 416 nm in the presence of RCDs were monitored to evaluate the photosensitizing capabilities of these RCDs after 635 nm laser illumination [42]. Fig. 2a and S4 showed that approximately 17%, 75%, and 100% DPBF degradation could be observed in the h-RCDs, e-RCDs, and p-RCDs groups, respectively, which suggests the sequentially enhanced ROS generation from h-RCDs to e-RCDs to p-RCDs.

To differentiate ROSs produced by RCDs, DHE was selected as the O2 −• specific fluorescence probe [43,44]. Fig. S5 exhibited that red fluorescence with maximum emission of approximately 605 nm could be detected. Moreover, the fluorescence intensities induced by h-RCDs, e-RCDs, and p-RCDs (Fig. 2 b) had similar enhancement, indicating their identical capabilities for O2 −• production. ABDA was employed to assess the capability of RCDs for 1O2 generation. As shown in Fig. 2c–f, the absorbance of ABDA at 380 nm decreased gradually with prolonged irradiation time. Approximately 12%, 60%, and 98% ABDA degradation could be observed in the h-RCDs, e-RCDs, and p-RCDs groups. This result illustrated the different 1O2 productions of the RCDs. The 1O2 quantum yields of the h-RCDs, e-RCDs, and p-RCDs were calculated to be 0.02, 0.18, and 0.42 using MB as the standard PS (Fig. S6). Finally, ESR was applied to evaluate the O2 −• and 1O2 generation using DMPO and TEMP. The three RCDs induced similar DMPO ESR signal intensities (Fig. 2g). By contrast, the ESR signal intensity of TEMPO progressively increased from h-RCDs to e-RCDs to p-RCDs (Fig. 2h). These results were consistent with the DHE and ABDA degradation experiments. Overall, as exhibited in Fig. 2i, the as-prepared RCDs possessed equivalent O2 −• production via type I path; but 1O2 production sequentially increased from h-RCDs to e-RCDs to p-RCDs via type II path.

To understand their ROS generation mechanism, the excited state energy levels of RCDs were calculated by obtaining the maximum emission peaks of fluorescence and phosphorescence obtained at 77 K (Fig. 3 a–c) [45]. The maximum emission peaks of fluorescence and phosphorescence are located at approximately 661 nm and 706 nm for h-RCDs, 662 nm and 687 nm for e-RCDs, and 658 nm and 673 nm for p-RCDs. The simplified Jablonski diagrams in Fig. 3d present that the ΔE S1-T1 values of the h-RCDs, e-RCDs, and p-RCDs were 0.12, 0.07, and 0.04 eV, respectively. A smaller ΔE S1-T1 is more beneficial to the energy transfer from S1 to T1 and generated a more sensitized T1 state; thus, it makes the energy transfer to the adjacent O2 for 1O2 generation more efficient [46,47]. Therefore, it is suggested that the increasing 1O2 productions of RCDs are contributed to their reducing ΔE S1-T1 (Fig. 3e).

Upon irradiation, the photoexcited electrons are promoted from the valence band (E v) to the conduction band (E c). Subsequently, the generated electrons could react with the surrounding O2 to produce O2 −• [48]. In general, the reducing ability of electrons in the E c plays a decisive role in O2 −• formation. Therefore, the E c of the RCDs were measured by UPS. Firstly, WF can be achieved by subtracting the secondary electron cutoff energy (E
cutoff ) from 21.22 eV. The E
cutoff of RCDs were 15.79 eV, 15.94 eV, and 16.05 eV. Therefore, the WF were calculated to be 5.43 eV, 5.28 eV, 5.17 eV, respectively (Fig. 3f, left) [49]. And Ev with respect to the Fermi level of RCDs were calculated to be approximately 1.54 eV, 1.69 eV, and 1.80 eV, respectively (Fig. 3f, right), Ev with respect to vacuum level of three RCDs were further calculated to be approximately 6.97 eV (Fig. 3g). According to the UV–vis spectra, the band gaps of RCDs (Eg) were calculated to be approximately 1.87 eV. Thus, with respect to the vacuum level, the conduction band (Ec) could be calculated to be approximately 5.1 eV. Based on these data, the vacuum Ec values of the RCDs were converted into normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) based on the reference standard for which 0 eV vacuum level versus equals −4.44 eV NHE, the Ec values of all RCDs could be finally calculated to be approximately −0.66 eV, which were much lesser than the redox potentials (E H) of O2/O2 −• (−0.2 eV). This finding indicated that all RCDs could transfer equal electrons to O2 to produce identical O2 −• yields.

The TEM, FITR, and XPS results suggest that the three RCDs have similar core structures and different surface states, while their surface states were dominated by –OH and –COOH, and the CO content of e-RCDs and pyridinic N content of p-RCDs are all incremental. Thus, we designed three quasi structures corresponding to three RCDs, as shown in Fig. 3h. The theoretical calculation was performed to analyze the correlation between the excited state energy levels and surface states of the RCDs. Specifically, the ground state geometry was optimized using density functional theory (DFT), and then the excited states were calculated with linear response time-dependent DFT (TD-DFT) at the optimized ground state geometry. All calculations were performed with the hybrid B3LYP function and the 6-311G* basis set [50–52]. The results indicated that the ΔE S1-T1 values of the three RCDs were 0.71, 0.57, and 0.45 eV, respectively, agreeing well with the gradual decreasing trend measured above. The results also confirmed that rationally adjusting the surface states resulted in different ΔE S1-T1, and a smaller ΔE S1-T1 is conducive to produce more 1O2. Meanwhile, the equal O2 −• generation efficiency attributes to the same core structures of the RCDs.

To assess the in vitro anticancer efficacy of RCDs, 4T1 cells incubated with the three RCDs were irradiated with 635 nm laser at 100 mW cm−2 for 10 min and then co-stained with calcein-AM/PI. As exhibited in Fig. 4b and S10 , only green fluorescence was observed in the control group or RCDs without light irradiation groups. However, the green fluorescence decreased gradually, whereas the red fluorescence increased gradually from h-RCDs to e-RCDs, and a homogeneous red fluorescence could be observed in the p-RCDs, indicating the complete killing of tumor cells owing to the higher ROS production of p-RCDs.

MTT assay was further used to calculate the mortality of 4T1 cells incubated with RCDs (Fig. 4 c). As expected, no remarkable cytotoxicity was observed in the three RCDs groups in dark, indicating the good biocompatibility of RCDs. However, the mortality of 4T1 cells gradually increased with increasing RCDs concentration upon 635 nm laser irradiation. Approximately 52% mortality was realized by h-RCDs at 200 μg mL−1, and higher mortalities up to 70% and 95% were achieved in e-RCDs and p-RCDs groups, respectively. The anticancer effects of the RCDs against 4T1 cells under hypoxic environment were also investigated. Fig. S11 showed that the mortalities of 4T1 cells induced by 200 μg mL−1 RCDs were all nearly 50%, suggesting the identical type I photodynamic effect of the RCDs, and showed the potentials of type I/II RCDs for PDT in hypoxic microenvironment. In addition, DHE and SOSG probes were used to monitor the intracellular O2 −• and 1O2 production under hypoxic conditions (Figs. S12 and S13). The CLSM results indicated that the DHE-treated groups showed bright red fluorescence under hypoxia upon laser irradiation, whereas no obvious fluorescence was observed in SOSG-treated groups. Importantly, the remarkable green fluorescence of HPF for •OH detection were detected in 4T1 cells under normoxia (Fig. S14) or hypoxia (Fig. S15). The results confirmed that type II PDT would be deactivated, and type I PDT would play the dominant role in killing tumor under hypoxia [11,53,54].

To clarify the anticancer mechanism induced by the RCDs, the Annexin V-FITC/PI apoptosis detection kit was performed by flow cytometry (Fig. 4d). Except for the control group, the 4T1 cells in the RCDs groups migrated from Q4 (living cells) to Q3 (early apoptosis) and Q2 (late apoptosis), revealing their apoptosis-activated cell death pathway. The percentage of apoptosis in p-RCDs treated group was approximately 96%, which was higher than those of the e-RCDs (76.2%) and h-RCDs (44.9%). Similarly, CLSM with Annexin V and PI staining revealed the incremental apoptosis-induced cell death from h-RCDs to e-RCDs and to p-RCDs (Fig. S16). The results indicated that the apoptosis-induced mortality rate caused by PDT with p-RCDs was higher than those caused by h-RCDs and e-RCDs, which were consistent with the results of the calcein- AM/PI co-staining and MTT assay.

Staining 4T1 cells with RCDs led to strong photoluminescence emission from the cells. The fluorescence images in Fig. S17 demonstrated that the RCDs labeled only the cytoplasm and not the nucleus. Co-localization experiments were further performed to investigate the specific cellular target of the RCDs using the commercial fluorescent dyes, Lyso-Tracker green and Mito-Tracker green, as the lysosomal and mitochondrial indicators, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5 a–b, CLSM images revealed that the p-RCDs incubated with 4T1 cells for 3 h mostly gathered in the lysosomes (R p = 93%) and less gathered in the mitochondria (R p = 57%). However, the p-RCDs accumulated in the mitochondria (R p = 96%) when the incubation time was prolonged to 8 h. Similarly, Figs. S18 and S19 indicated that h-RCDs and e-RCDs exhibited the same pathway as the p-RCDs for targeting the mitochondria. All results suggested that the RCDs initially entered the lysosome through endocytosis and then escaped the lysosome into the mitochondria.

The stability of mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP) is an important guarantee for cell metabolism, and its imbalance causes a change in mitochondrial membrane permeability [55,56]. In normal mitochondria, JC-1 could accumulate in the mitochondrial matrix to form a polymer, which emits strong red fluorescence; when the membrane potential of damaged mitochondria decreased or lost, JC-1 can produce green fluorescence in the form of monomer. Therefore, we used JC-1 to detect the damage of RCDs toward the MMP of 4T1 cells. As indicated in Fig. 5c, the 4T1 cells without RCDs treatment showed strong red fluorescence from JC-1. However, the RCDs-treated 4T1 cells with 635 nm laser irradiation exhibited gradually enhanced green fluorescence from h-RCDs to e-RCDs and p-RCDs, suggesting that the mitochondria of the 4T1 cells were damaged by the RCDs upon irradiation.

The release of cytochrome c, one of the most common respiratory-chain proteins located in the mitochondria, into the cytosol is the hallmark of apoptosis after the loss of MMP [57]. Thus, the distribution changes of cytochrome c were measured using CLSM. As shown in Fig. 5d, compared with the control group, cytochrome c (labeled as green) in the RCDs-treated 4T1 cells gradually released from the mitochondria (labeled as red) to the cytosol, especially in p-RCDs-treated group. The release of cytochrome c can activate caspase-9 located in the intermembrane of the mitochondria. In addition, the cleaved caspase 9 could also activate caspase 3, and then degrade poly (ADP-ribose) polymerase 1 (PARP) from 116 kDa to 85 kDa, resulting in its incapability for repairing DNA and maintaining genome integrity [58,59]. As shown in Fig. 5e and f, the expressions of cleaved caspase 9, cleaved caspase 3, and PARP in p-RCDs treated group detected by Western Blot were significantly higher than those treated with e-RCDs and h-RCDs groups.

In brief, during the photoactived CDs-induced anticancer, the as-prepared RCDs initially entered the lysosome through endocytosis (step 1) and then escaped the lysosome into the mitochondria (step 2). Upon photoactivation, the damaged mitochondria released cytochrome c (step 3) and initiated the cascade caspases, which induced apoptosis signal transduction. The overexpression of cleaved caspase-9 (step 4) subsequently activated the effector of caspase-3 (step 5), which can deactivate PARP (step 6), and led to cell apoptosis (step 7), as illustrated in Fig. 5g.

Considering their near-infrared emission around 680 nm, in vivo fluorescence imaging capability of RCDs was investigated. Fig. 6a and S20 showed that strong fluorescence signals of RCDs in 4T1 tumors could be obviously collected, suggesting their potential for imaging-guided PDT.

Inspired by the promising PDT efficacy in vitro, the anticancer performance of the RCDs in vivo was investigated using nude mice bearing 4T1 tumor as the models. The 4T1 tumor-bearing mice were randomly divided into four groups (n = 3): PBS as the control (group I), h-RCDs (group II), e-RCDs (group III), and p-RCDs (group IV). The growth rates of the tumors were measured every 2 days after 635 nm laser irradiation for 10 min. As shown in Fig. 6 b–d, the biggest tumor volume and the most rapid tumor growth could be observed in group I, whereas, tapering tumor volume and tumor growth were found in groups II and III. No obvious tumor growth was observed in group IV, indicating the complete growth inhibition of the tumor with p-RCDs due to their higher ROS generation via type I/II PDT. Furthermore, the H&E stained tumor slices demonstrated normal tumor morphology in group I, and severe damage with condensed nuclei in cancer cells in group IV, indicating the typical characteristics of apoptosis (Fig. 6e, upper). The TUNEL immunofluorescence results also verified the apoptotic cells in all groups and more positive signals in group IV than in groups II and III (Fig. 6e, lower). Moreover, no body-weight loss occurred (Fig. 6f), and no obvious damage in major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) could be observed (Fig. S21), illustrating the good biocompatibility of the RCDs. Furthermore, the blood routine and biochemical results indicated no significant difference in all parameters of RCDs treated groups compared to the control group, further suggesting their excellent biocompatibility (Figs. S22 and S23).

In this work, the type I/II RCD nanoPSs with equal O2 −• and incremental 1O2 generation were successfully prepared. The unaltered core structures of RCDs endowed their identical redox potentials, leading to the equal O2 −• production; the increase of 1O2 generation was proved to be attributed to the change of surface states (the increasing levels of CO and pyridinic N), which can promote the efficiency of ISC from S1 state to T1 state. Due to the effective accumulation in mitochondria, the well-designed RCDs could oxidatively destruct the key energy production process of tumor cells and activate their apoptotic pathways via caspase activation. In vitro and in vivo experiments demonstrated that RCDs can be used as novel nanoPSs for deep red fluorescence imaging-guided type I/II PDT. The achievement of highly efficient anticancer outcomes mainly ascribes to the following reasons: 1) O2 −• production via type I path and 1O2 generation via type II path contribute to approximately 50% cancer cell killing rate, respectively, and 2) the inherent mitochondrial targeting capability of RCDs enable them to be precision bombs toward tumors. We believe that this work would provide new prospects for designing and developing versatile CD nanoPSs with controllable ROS generation to conquer the limitation of current PDT and show considerable potential in biomedical applications.
